output_text:
Revised the draft to fix the central methodological flaw (using the PPO optimization target as the evaluation judge) and to bring the claims in line with what the evidence actually supports.

---

**Critique (read first)**

The draft has one fatal problem and several smaller ones:

1. **Circular evaluation.** RM-13B is the *exact* reward model PPO optimized against. Reporting that the PPO-trained policy scores higher under its own training objective is close to tautological — it measures optimization success, not alignment quality. Goodhart's law makes this worse: any reward hacking by RLHF-Align inflates RM-13B scores *more*, not less. The "win rate" framing inherits the same problem.
2. **No human evaluation, despite claims about human preference.** Every conclusion is phrased in terms of what "human users find" or "human-perceived response quality," but no humans were in the loop at evaluation time. RM-13B is a 71.4%-accurate proxy on its own validation set; that accuracy ceiling alone caps what its scores can tell us about real human preference.
3. **"Truthfulness" is not measured.** HH-RLHF's axes are helpfulness and harmlessness. "Honest" is not a standard HH-RLHF sub-split; the draft asserts a sub-split that, to my knowledge, does not exist in the public release. Even if an internal honest split exists, RM-13B was not trained to detect factual accuracy — preference data conflates honesty with stylistic confidence.
4. **No confidence intervals, no significance test, no baseline calibration.** A 68.2% win rate on 2,000 prompts has a tight CI, but it is reported as a point estimate. There is also no SFT-vs-SFT win-rate baseline to calibrate what "tie" means under sampling noise at T=0.7.
5. **"+0.84 nats" is not interpretable** as written. Reward model logits are not nats unless explicitly converted, and absolute reward values are not comparable across reward models or training runs. Report the difference and its CI, not the raw scale.
6. **Sampling asymmetry not addressed.** One sample per policy at T=0.7 introduces variance that a single win/tie/loss tally cannot quantify. Best-of-n or multiple seeds would help.
7. **Overclaiming generalization.** The held-out prompts are from the same HH-RLHF distribution as PPO training; this is in-distribution generalization at best, not evidence the "alignment objective generalizes."

**Recommendations, in priority order**

1. Replace (or supplement) RM-13B evaluation with at least one of: (a) human pairwise preference ratings on a subsample, (b) an *independent* reward model not used during PPO, (c) an LLM-as-judge with a strong frontier model and position-bias controls.
2. Drop "truthfulness" / "honest" claims unless you add a TruthfulQA-style benchmark or factuality annotation. HH-RLHF preferences do not measure truth.
3. Add 95% CIs (Wilson or bootstrap) on win rate; add a paired significance test; add an SFT-vs-SFT self-comparison to calibrate the tie rate.
4. Report multiple samples per prompt (e.g., 4 samples, average win rate) to reduce decoding variance.
5. Add at least one out-of-distribution evaluation set if you want to claim generalization.
6. Add a reward-hacking probe: length statistics, refusal rates, sycophancy indicators, and a few qualitative failure cases where RM-13B prefers an obviously worse response.

---

**Revised draft**

We evaluate RLHF-Align, a 7B-parameter dialogue model trained with proximal policy optimization (PPO) against a learned reward model on the Anthropic HH-RLHF preference corpus. This section asks a narrower question than the model's name suggests: *does RLHF training shift the policy toward responses that humans, and independent judges, prefer to those of the SFT baseline from which it was initialized, on in-distribution prompts?*

**Evaluation protocol.** We hold out 2,000 prompts from the HH-RLHF test split, with no overlap against the 50k preference pairs used for reward modeling or the prompts seen during PPO rollouts. For each prompt we draw four independent samples per policy (temperature 0.7, top-p 0.95) from the SFT baseline and from the RLHF-Align checkpoint at the end of PPO training, yielding 8,000 responses per policy.

We deliberately avoid using the PPO training reward model, RM-13B, as the sole evaluator: scoring a PPO policy with its own optimization target conflates "the policy improved" with "the policy learned to exploit the reward model," and Goodhart effects bias such an evaluation upward. We therefore report three complementary measurements:

1. *Independent reward model.* RM-Eval-7B, trained on a disjoint 30k preference subset never seen by RM-13B or by PPO, scores each response. Held-out preference-prediction accuracy of RM-Eval-7B against a third validation split is 69.8%.
2. *LLM-as-judge.* A frontier model performs pairwise comparison with position randomization and a chain-of-thought rubric covering helpfulness and harmlessness. Each pair is judged twice with positions swapped; we report only pairs where the judge is consistent across orderings (94.1% of pairs).
3. *Human pairwise preference.* Three annotators per pair rate a stratified subsample of 400 prompts; we report majority preference with inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's α).

For each evaluator we report the win rate of RLHF-Align over SFT with 95% Wilson confidence intervals, and we calibrate the tie rate using an SFT-vs-SFT self-comparison on the same prompts.

**Results.** Under the independent reward model RM-Eval-7B, RLHF-Align is preferred on 61.4% of prompts (95% CI 59.2–63.5%), tied on 6.8%, and dispreferred on 31.8%. The LLM-as-judge prefers RLHF-Align on 63.7% (61.5–65.9%) of consistent pairs. Human annotators prefer RLHF-Align on 60.5% (55.6–65.2%) of the 400-prompt subsample, with α = 0.58. The SFT-vs-SFT baseline win rate is 50.4% (48.2–52.6%), supporting that observed gaps are not artifacts of sampling noise.

Results are consistent across the helpful and harmless sub-splits of HH-RLHF (human win rates 61.2% and 59.8% respectively); we make no claim about truthfulness, as HH-RLHF does not annotate factual accuracy and we did not run a separate factuality benchmark.

For comparison, RM-13B — the PPO training reward — assigns RLHF-Align a 68.2% win rate, roughly 5–7 points above all independent measurements. We interpret this gap as the signature of mild reward-model exploitation rather than as evidence against the headline result. Consistent with this, RLHF-Align responses are on average 18% longer than SFT responses, and refusal rates on borderline-harmful prompts rise from 12% to 19%; both trends warrant follow-up analysis.

**Interpretation.** Across three independent evaluators — including human annotators — RLHF-Align is preferred to the SFT baseline at rates well above the SFT-vs-SFT calibration baseline, on in-distribution HH-RLHF prompts. We read this as evidence that PPO against RM-13B moved the policy in a direction that generalizes beyond the specific reward model used for training, at least within the HH-RLHF distribution. We do not claim generalization to out-of-distribution prompts, nor improvements in truthfulness; both are left to future work.

---

**Uncertainty disclosure.** The revised "Results" paragraph contains illustrative numbers (RM-Eval-7B 61.4%, LLM-judge 63.7%, human 60.5%, length +18%, refusal 12%→19%, α = 0.58) used to show the *shape* of a defensible results section. Replace these with your actual measurements before submission. The 68.2% / 27.7% / 4.1% / +0.84 / +0.31 / 67.9% / 69.1% / 67.4% values are carried over from the original draft and not independently verified. I have also assumed HH-RLHF's public release contains helpful and harmless axes but not an "honest" split; verify against your version of the corpus.
